coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorescent visual detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate via blue fluorescent carbon dots and berberine chloride hydrate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Zhen
Dong, Hongcen
Liang, Jiaman
Zhang, Fang
Chen, Xianping
Du, Lingling
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: As a kind of emerging persistent organic pollutants, perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and its salts have caused global ecosystem pollution. To develop rapid, sensitive and low-cost detection method of PFOS is of great importance. In this work, a novel sensing method has been proposed for the highly selective fluorescent visual detection of PFOS in aqueous solution based on carbon dots (CDs) and berberine chloride hydrate (BH). It was found that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of berberine chloride hydrate in pH 6.09 Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solution. When PFOS was added to the system, the fluorescence was restored slightly at 448 nm and enhanced apparently at 533 nm, but no phenomenon occurred with other perfluorinated compounds. As a consequence, an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation (from blue to light yellow) of solution was observed. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the enhanced fluorescence intensities at 533 nm are in proportion to the concentration of PFOS in the range of 0.22–50.0 μmol/L (R2 = 0.9919), with a detection limit of 21.7 nmol/L (3σ). The proposed approach has been successfully applied to the detection of PFOS in environmental water samples with RSD ≤ 1.1%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)
Carbon dots (CDs)
Berberine chloride hydrate (BH)
Selectivity
Fluorescent visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH and Buffer Solutions
The Mechanism of Interaction Between BH and CDs
PFOS Sensing Based on CDs and BH
The Mechanism of Interaction Between PFOS and CDs-BH
Selectivity and the Effects of Foreign Ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of carbon dots. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of carbon dots. Inset is a photo of the carbon dots under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (B) fluorescence spectroscopy of carbon dots with excitation wavelength changing from 320 to 390 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Effect of berberine chloride hydrate on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/BH and CDs/BH/PFOS. CDs, 60 μL; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; pH 6.09. (B) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/BH, CDs/BH/PFOS and CDs/BH/PFOA. CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; PFOA, 5 μmol/L. (C) Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/BH and CDs/BH/PFOS. CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; pH 6.09. (D) Effect of time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/BH and CDs/BH/PFOS. CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; pH 6.09. (E) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/BH and CDs/BH/PFOS. CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; pH 6.09.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of BH at various concentrations of 0–40 μmol/L. The inset shows that the fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs-PFOA and CDs-PFOS, respectively. a, CDs-PFOS; b, CDs; c, CDs-PFOA. Concentration: CDs, 60 μL; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; PFOA, 5 μmol/L. (B) The corresponding calibration curve. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of BH. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-BH in the presence of PFOS at various concentrations of 0–50 μmol/L. (D) The corresponding calibration curve. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BH in the absence and presence of PFOS. (E) The photo of FL color change of CDs/CDs-BH at various concentrations of PFOS. The concentrations of PFOS from left to right are 0, 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 and 50 μmol/L, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence response of CDs-BH system towards various perfluorinated compounds. Concentration: CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; the concentrations of various perfluorinated compounds are 5 μmol/L. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BH in the absence and presence of various perfluorinated compounds.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) the tap water spiked with different concentrations of PFOS and (B) the Jialing River water spiked with different concentrations of PFOS (0, 5, 10, and 15 μmol/L). (C) and (D) Visual color change of the spiked tap water and Jialing River water, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; PFOS, 5 μmol/L; pH 6.09.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of PFOS in real water samples using the proposed method (n = 5).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Sample2: Jialing River, the water samples were taken from the Jialing River in Beibei Dock. The PFOS in all water samples are hardly detected by the present method. CDs, 60 μL; BH, 40 μmol/L; pH 6.09.

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorescent visual detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate via blue fluorescent carbon dots and berberine chloride hydrate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Class 12 Senior Grade 2018, High School Affiliated to Southwest University, Chongqing 400700, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: High School Affiliated to Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a kind of emerging persistent organic pollutants, perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and its salts have caused global ecosystem pollution. To develop rapid, sensitive and low-cost detection method of PFOS is of great importance. In this work, a novel sensing method has been proposed for the highly selective fluorescent visual detection of PFOS in aqueous solution based on carbon dots (CDs) and berberine chloride hydrate (BH). It was found that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of berberine chloride hydrate in pH 6.09 Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solution. When PFOS was added to the system, the fluorescence was restored slightly at 448 nm and enhanced apparently at 533 nm, but no phenomenon occurred with other perfluorinated compounds. As a consequence, an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation (from blue to light yellow) of solution was observed. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the enhanced fluorescence intensities at 533 nm are in proportion to the concentration of PFOS in the range of 0.22–50.0 μmol/L (R2 = 0.9919), with a detection limit of 21.7 nmol/L (3σ). The proposed approach has been successfully applied to the detection of PFOS in environmental water samples with RSD ≤ 1.1%.

A novel sensing method for fluorescent visual and turn-on detection of PFOS via blue fluorescent carbon dots and berberine chloride hydrate (BH) has been developed.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Perfluorinated compounds (PFCs) are a class of fully fluorinated chemicals, which are mainly composed of hydrophobic carbon chain and hydrophilic end groups [1]. Since 1950s, PFCs have been widely used in industrial and civil fields, such as firefighting foams, adhesives, refrigerants, shampoos, floor polishes and cleaners [2–4]. In PFCs, perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) are the most widely studied and representative [1,5,6]. As an emerging persistent organic pollutant, PFOS can enter the atmosphere, water, sludge, soil and other environmental media, and it has strong resistance to photolysis, hydrolysis and biodegradation in environmental medium. So, once PFOS enters the environment, it will be difficult to break down [7]. At the same time, PFOS can be transferred, bioaccumulated and biomagnified along the food chains and has been found in the serum, liver and bladder of human beings and animals around the world, including the Arctic [8,9]. PFOS is toxic and can cause harm to biological organisms. Studies have shown that PFOS can cause hepatotoxicity [10,11], development toxicity [12,13], immunotoxicity [14] or neurotoxicity [15] in human and animals. In view of the environmental pollution and health hazards caused by PFOS, in May 2000, 3M, the principal manufacturer of PFOS, announced it would voluntarily give up producing PFOS and its related substances [16]. In 2001, PFOS has been listed in the blacklist of persistent organic pollutants by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). So, it is very significative to find a simple and fast PFOS detection technology.

At present, many methods for PFOS analysis have been proposed, such as liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [17,18], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [19,20], capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) [21], liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS–MS) [22–24], etc. Among the above methods, the sensitivity of CZE is lower. Although LC–MS, GC–MS and LC–MS–MS have a high sensitivity, these methods require expensive instruments, complicated operation and long analysis period. Therefore, it is necessary and important to develop a simple, inexpensive and rapid method for the determination of PFOS.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of harmless and eco-friendly fluorescent nanoparticles, compared with the traditional metal quantum dots. In recent years, more and more researchers have begun to research and develop carbon dots. Because they exhibit stable photoluminescence, high photostability, low cost, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and good water solubility [25,26], carbon dots, an ideal material, used as fluorescent probes, are widely applied to biochemical analysis [27,28], cell imaging [29,30], drug detection [31,32], etc. A large number of applications of carbon dots have been reported, related to metal ions (Cu2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, etc.) [33–35], anion [36] and biomolecule [37] and so on. Up to now, the reported fluorescent probes for PFOS or PFOA are based on fluorescence quenching response [38,39]. For fluorescent detection of PFOS, fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) is preferable to fluorescence quenching (turn-off), since the former reduces the chance of false positive data by other fluorescent quenchers existing in samples [40,41]. And fluorescent visual detection of PFOS has almost not been reported.

Based on the above, we designed a method for fluorescent visual and turn-on detection of PFOS via blue fluorescent carbon dots and berberine chloride hydrate (BH). As shown in Scheme 1 , CDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence in solution. Berberine chloride hydrate (BH), a kind of alkaloid and antibacterial drug, can bind with CDs and then quench the fluorescence of CDs. When PFOS was added to CDs-BH system, the fluorescence was restored slightly at 448 nm and enhanced apparently at 533 nm. And an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation (from blue to light yellow) of solution was observed. The peak at 533 nm is weak fluorescence peak of BH, and PFOS can enhance fluorescence of BH. The proposed method has been applied to detecting PFOS in real water samples.

The fluorescence spectra and absorption spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The TEM images of CDs were obtained with a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The functional groups of CDs were recorded through a Shimadzu FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was obtained with an FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). Zeta potentials of system were determined through a ZEN3600 dynamic laser light scattering (Malvern, English). A FE20/EL20 pH meter (Shanghai, China) was used to measure the pH value of the solution and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was used to blend the solution. LGJ-10C freeze drier (Beijing, China) was used to freeze and dry CDs. Electric thermostatic drier DGH-9036A (Shanghai, China) was used to synthetize CDs.

Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS, 98.0%), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA, 98.0%), nonafluorobutane‑1‑sulfonic acid (PFBS, 98.0%), heptafluorobutyric acid (PFBA, 98.0%), pentafluoropropionic acid (PFPrA, 98.0%), nonafluorovaleric acid (PFPeA, 98.0%), undecafluorohexanoic acid (PFHeA, 98.0%), tridecafluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA, 98.0%) and heptadecafluorononanoic acid (PFNA, 95.0%) were obtained from Shanghai Tixiai Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). (3‑Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMOS, 97%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95.0–98.0%) and berberine chloride hydrate (BH, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Britton-Robinson (BR, H3PO4-HAc-H3BO3-NaOH) buffer solution (pH 6.09) was used to control the acidity of the system. All other routine reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification, and ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was used throughout.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method [42]. Briefly, 850 μL APTMOS and 50 μL H2SO4 (0.05 mol/L) were mixed and diluted by water to 4 mL. The mixture was put into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated for 9 h at 190 °C in a drying oven. After the reaction was finished, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. Then, the obtained light yellow solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to separate large particles and the supernatant was collected. For purification, pH of the carbon quantum dots solution was adjusted to 7 by 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 and the product was dialyzed through a dialysis tube (1000 Da, molecular weight cut off) in the dark for 24 h to remove unreacted APTMOS, SO4 2− and small molecule hydrolysis products. Finally, the purified CDs solution was freeze-dried into a powder.

The procedure for PFOS detection is described as follows: 60 μL CDs and 200 μL BR buffer solution (pH 6.09) were added into a 2 mL microtube. After mixed, 80 μL of 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L BH was added. The mixture was mixed again. Then an appropriate volume of PFOS solution was added. After mixed, the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water and then mixed thoroughly. The mixture was placed for 10 min at room temperature and then transferred for fluorescence and absorbance measurements.

The optical properties of the as-prepared carbon dots were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1A, it shows that the carbon dots have an obvious absorption peak at 270 nm, which is due to the π-π* transition of carbon nanoparticles [43]. Fluorescence spectrum of CDs shows a 448 nm maximum emission peak under the maximum excitation of 360 nm. The inset shows CDs solution presents light yellow under visible light and bright blue under 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. 1B, it was found that the carbon dots have the properties of excitation-dependent emission, that is, the maximum emission wavelength is changed with the change of the excitation wavelength. This property of excitation-dependent emission is similar to previous reports, which might be due to the inhomogeneity or surface defects of carbon dots [43,44].

TEM image was used to study the morphology of CDs. As can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. S1A, CDs are mono-dispersed and homogeneous. The particle size of CDs is mainly distributed in 0.6–1.8 nm. The average particle size of CDs is 1.12 nm. As shown in Fig. S1B, FT-IR spectrum was acquired to study the functional groups of CDs. The stretching vibrations of OH and COOH could be observed at 3429.43 cm−1 and 1631.78 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic absorption band of NHX, NH, SiOSi and SiOC (3055.24 cm−1, 1527.62 cm−1, 964.41 cm−1 and 1111.00 cm−1) were all observed in the FT-IR spectra of the CDs. The absorption peaks at 1053.13 cm−1 and 2935.66 cm−1 were related to COH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. Based on the above, it can be found that CDs are mainly surrounded by hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups. The presence of these functional hydrophilic groups on the surface greatly improved the water solubility of the CDs.
In order to obtain the optimal conditions for the detection of PFOS, the influences of various factors on the system were investigated by a series of experiments.

The concentration of BH is a key parameter that should be taken into consideration in order to improve the sensitivity. So the optimal concentration of BH was studied. The results were displayed in Fig. 2A. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BH system decreases, with the increase of BH concentration. When the concentration of BH is below 40 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BH-PFOS system increases first and then remains unchanged with the increase of BH concentration. However, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BH-PFOS system decreases when the BH is above 40 μmol/L. The enhanced fluorescence intensity (F-F0) and the intensity percentage [(F-F0)/F0] reach the maximum, and the background signal is low when the BH is 40 μmol/L. Hence, 40 μmol/L BH was chosen as the optimum condition for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and Buffer Solutions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get a higher sensitivity, the impacts of buffers (BR, Citric acid-Sodium citrate, Tris-HCl) on the fluorescence intensity of the system were studied. As shown in Fig. 2B and Fig. S2, the enhanced fluorescence intensity (F-F0) is similar in the three buffers, but the fluorescence enhancement efficiency [(F-F0)/F0] is the maximum in the BR buffer solution. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BH in the absence and presence of PFOS. And the background signal is also lower in the BR buffer solution. Therefore, BR buffer solution is the best choice due to high sensitivity of the system. The influence of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of the system was investigated. Fig. 2B shows fluorescence intensity of the system is constant and not affected by pH. And the fluorescence intensity of the system is more stable at pH 5.02–7.0. So, BR buffer solution of pH 6.09 was used to control the acidity of the system. Meanwhile, the impacts of PFOA, another representative of Perfluorinated compounds, on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BH system were also studied under the same condition. Fig. 2B indicates the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BH system almost has no response to PFOA in the BR buffer medium. The proposed assay demonstrates higher sensitivity to PFOS than PFOA, which might be caused by the higher hydrophobicity of PFOS than PFOA [45] and higher electrostatic interaction based on PFOS with BH than PFOA [46]. Thus, PFOS and PFOA can be distinguished successfully.

Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the system was studied. As can be seen in Fig. 2C, NaCl has no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BH and CDs-BH-PFOS system. In order to simplify the operation process and save reagent, NaCl is not added to the system.

The influence of reaction time was tested. The result (Fig. 2D) indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the system reaches a maximum after reacting about 10 min and remains constant at least 2 h. Hence, the subsequent fluorescence measurement was conducted after 10 min of reaction.

The influences of temperature ranging from 27 °C to 65 °C on the fluorescence intensity of the system were tested. As shown in Fig. 2E, when the temperature is below 40 °C, the system is not affected by temperature. When the temperature is above 40 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the system decreases. It is because high temperature increases the collision of molecules, which makes the collision probability of excited molecules and solvent molecules increase, leading to the decrease of fluorescent intensity [47]. Therefore, all experiments were carried out at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 3A, it was found that the fluorescence of carbon dots at 448 nm could be quenched by the berberine chloride hydrate. It was clear that F0-F exhibited a linear response to the BH concentration in the range of 0.5–40 μmol/L (Fig. 3B). The fitted linear data could be expressed as F0-F = 19.0604 + 9.3715c (BH, μmol/L), R2 = 0.9961. Detection limit for the determination of BH was 50 nmol/L (3σ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Mechanism of Interaction Between BH and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S3A, the excitation spectra of CDs has three bands at 239, 301 and 360 nm, and the emission spectra of CDs under the excitation of 360 nm is located at 448 nm. However, BH exhibits broad absorption at 227, 263, 343 and 422 nm, respectively, showing good overlapping with the excitation and emission bands of CDs. Thus, BH can not only shied the excitation light for CDs but also absorb the emission light from CDs. As shown in Fig. S3B, after the addition CDs into BH, the absorption intensity of BH obviously enhances. The absorption enhancement of BH could be successfully converted to fluorescence quenching of CDs, which ensures that the inner filter effect (IFE) occurs [48].

As shown in Fig. S3C, it was found that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs remained unchanged, before and after introducing BH to CDs aqueous solution. Therefore, BH has no effect on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, which indicates that the mechanism of the quenching process involves static quenching rather than dynamic quenching [49]. Another way to distinguish static and dynamic quenching is to investigate the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore. Collisional quenching only affects the excited states of the fluorophores, and the absorption spectra of the fluorophore has no change. On the contrary, ground-state complex formation will frequently lead to perturbation of the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore [50]. In order to reconfirm the possible quenching mechanism (ground-state complex formation) of fluorescence of CDs by BH, the absorption spectrums of CDs, BH and CDs-BH were investigated (Fig. S3B). The absorption of CDs is characterized by a board band at 270 nm. The addition of BH led to increase at 270 nm in the CDs absorption. Compared to absorption of BH, absorption of CDs-BH increased. These results reconfirm that the probable quenching mechanism of fluorescence of CDs by BH is a static quenching procedure.

In addition, the zeta potential of the system was also studied. After BH was added into CDs solution at pH 6.09, the zeta potential of CDs changed from −4.78 mV to −1.68 mV. The result shows the electrostatic interaction plays an important role in quenching procedure of fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PFOS Sensing Based on CDs and BH
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3C, after different amount of PFOS was added to the CDs-BH system, the fluorescence was restored slightly at 448 nm and enhanced apparently at 533 nm. As a consequence, an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation (from blue to light yellow) of solution was observed (Fig. 3E). Compared with BH-PFOS (Fig. S4A) system (colorless to yellowish green), CDs-BH-PFOS system has better visualization effect (Higher recognition of the naked eye). Based on the above, the fluorescent visual and turn-on detection of PFOS fluorescence analysis method is established. Under optimal conditions, as shown in Fig. 3D, the enhanced fluorescence intensity (F-F0) for PFOS at 533 nm can be fitted to the equation of F-F0 = −18.5203 + 42.3016c (PFOS, μmol/L) over the range of 0.22 to 50 μmol/L with a detection limit (3σ) of 21.7 nmol/L (R2 = 0.9919). And there is also a linear relationship at 448 nm (Fig. S5). Fig. S5 shows that F-F0 exhibits a linear response to the PFOS concentration in the range of 0.8–25 μmol/L at 448 nm. The fitted linear data can be expressed as F-F0 = 15.1461 + 4.6345c (PFOS, μmol/L), R2 = 0.9950.

Meanwhile, the interaction of BH and PFOS was also studied. As can be seen in Fig. S4B, fluorescence signals of BH were very weak in the absence of PFOS. When trace amount of PFOS was added into BH solution, it can be seen that strong fluorescence signal at 533 nm can be observed under excitation of 360 nm. Fig. S4C shows the enhanced fluorescence intensity (F-F0) versus the concentration of PFOS. The fluorescence enhancement can be described by the following equations: F-F0 = −94.9274 + 43.9964c (PFOS, μmol/L). And a linear calibration was plotted ranging from 0.23 to 40 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient of 0.9918. The detection limit is 23.4 nmol/L. Fig. S4A shows that the fluorescence intensity of BH increases in the presence of PFOS (colorless to yellowish green).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Mechanism of Interaction Between PFOS and CDs-BH
ce:section.ce:para: The inset in Fig. 3A shows that CDs have no response to PFOS/PFOA and the fluorescence intensity of BH increases in the presence of PFOS. These results demonstrate that fluorescence enhancement of CDs-BH system in the presence of PFOS is due to the interaction of BH and PFOS. As shown in Fig. S3B, PFOS has no absorption. BH exhibits broad absorption at 227, 263, 343 and 422 nm, respectively. The absorption intensity of BH significantly decreased when PFOS was added to BH solution. Absorption intensity of CDs-BH also decreased at 227, 263 and 422 nm in the presence of PFOS, but absorption intensity of CDs-BH increased at 343 nm. The above results indicate BH may react with PFOS. BH, which is positively charged, can bond with PFOS, anionic surfactant, by electrostatic interaction [51].

Meanwhile, the zeta potential further confirmed it. It was found that, after PFOS was added into BH solution at pH 6.09, the zeta potential of BH changed from 3.32 mV to 1.16 mV. And zeta potential of CDs-BH system changed from −1.68 mV to −3.86 mV, after PFOS was added to CDs-BH solution at pH 6.09. Thus, it is speculated that the electrostatic interaction plays an essential role in the ion-pairing formation between BH and PFOS. Due to forming ion-pairing, the fluorescence of system enhances [52–55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and the Effects of Foreign Ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selectivity of the presented method, the effect of different perfluorinated compounds on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 , the system has almost no response to other perfluorinated compounds except PFOS under the same experimental conditions, which indicates that the proposed method can selectively detect PFOS.

The impacts of potentially co-existing substances on the detection of PFOS were also studied. As shown in Table 1 , common inorganic ions and EDTA can be allowed with high concentration, while few metal ions such as Ca2+, Hg2+ and Cd2+ are allowed at relatively low concentration. Although these ions have an effect on the system, they can be removed by cation exchange resin. Meanwhile, SDS and SDBS also have considerable impacts. But they can be removed by introducing excessive Ba2+ into the sample [56]. Excess Ba2+ can be removed by cation exchange resin.

The river water sample was obtained from the Jialing River in Beibei Dock. And the tap water was obtained from the lab. The samples were heated to boil, cooled and then filtered by 0.22 μm filterable membranes triple. To eliminate SDS and SDBS, an excess of Ba2+ was added into the samples. The samples were shocked and filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane twice. Cation exchange resin was then used to eliminate the cation. The pretreated water samples were analyzed according to the experimental process of detecting PFOS.

In order to investigate the potential application of the proposed method, simple, rapid and cost-effective visualization of PFOS in real samples was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 , no PFOS was detected in the tap water and Jialing River water samples, and the solution showed blue. However, the color changes were observed in the spiked tap water and Jialing River water samples upon the addition of different concentrations of PFOS (5, 10, and 15 μmol/L). Moreover, the yellow fluorescence intensity depended on the concentration of PFOS, which was validated by fluorescence emission spectra, suggesting the feasibility of the proposed method.

In addition, the recovery of the developed approach was studied. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery was 91.6–99.6%. The results demonstrated that the developed method was reliable and suitable for the visual detection of the targets in real samples.

In summary, an assay method for fluorescent visual and turn-on detection of PFOS was designed in this work. Berberine chloride hydrate can quench the fluorescence of CDs through forming a ground state complex, the inner filter effect (IFE) and the electrostatic interaction. Upon the addition of PFOS into CDs-BH solution, the fluorescence can be recovered slightly at 448 nm, and fluorescence at 533 nm enhanced apparently. As a consequence, an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation (from blue to light yellow) of solution was observed. The new construction platform is simple and rapid with low cost and high selectivity, which is able to distinguish between PFOA and PFOS and has been successfully applied to the detection of PFOS in environmental water samples with satisfactory results. The proposed method possesses the potential for evaluating the treatment process of PFOS in the water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21377103).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.09.028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pre- and postnatal exposure to perfluorinated compounds (PFCs)
Sources and fate of perfluorinated compounds in the aqueous environment and in drinking water of a highly urbanized and industrialized area in Italy
Induction of oxidative stress and apoptosis by PFOS and PFOA in primary cultured hepatocytes of freshwater tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
Concentrations of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) in vacuum cleaner dust collected in Japanese homes
Evaluation of perfluorinated compounds in seven wastewater treatment plants in Beijing urban areas
Indoor sources of poly- and perfluorinated compounds (PFCS) in Vancouver, Canada: implications for human exposure
Global distribution of perfluorooctane sulfonate in wildlife
Perfluorinated organic compounds in human blood serum and seminal plasma: a study of urban and rural tea worker populations in Sri Lanka
Chronic effects of water-borne PFOS exposure on growth, survival and hepatotoxicity in zebrafsh: a partial life-cycle test
Evaluation of the toxicological effects of perfluorooctane sulfonic acid in the common carp (Cyprinus carpio)
